model_narrative: We are choosing how to spread an investment budget across a set of candidate stocks, with the weight on each stock to be decided. We have historical daily returns for every stock over a window of trading days, along with each stock's average return. We want a portfolio whose day-to-day return stays as close as possible to its own average, so the goal is to minimize the total absolute deviation of the portfolio return across all the observed days.

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the candidate stocks that can be held in the portfolio

sets.name: d
sets.doc: the historical trading days over which returns are observed

params.name: mean
params.doc: the mean daily return of each stock over the observation window, as a fractional return

params.name: dev
params.doc: the deviation of a stock's return on a given day from that stock's own mean return, as a fractional return

params.name: totmean
params.doc: the overall average mean return across the candidate stocks, as a fractional return; it is negative for this data

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the portfolio weight allocated to each stock, as a fraction of the total budget

vars.name: wplus
vars.doc: the positive part of the portfolio return deviation on each day

vars.name: wmin
vars.doc: the negative part of the portfolio return deviation on each day

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the total absolute deviation of the portfolio return summed over all days

constraints.name: wplus[dd]
constraints.intent: For each observed day, the portfolio's return deviation that day is split into a positive part and a negative part. The positive part minus the negative part must equal the portfolio's overall deviation on that day, which is obtained by combining each stock's deviation from its own mean on that day weighted by how much of the budget is placed in that stock.

constraints.name: budget
constraints.intent: The entire budget must be fully allocated. The portfolio weights across all the candidate stocks must add up to one.

constraints.name: retcon
constraints.intent: The portfolio must achieve a minimum level of expected return. The budget-weighted average of the stocks' mean returns must be at least one and a quarter times the overall average mean return.

constraints.name: zdef
constraints.intent: The total absolute deviation of the portfolio is the running total of its daily deviation magnitudes. It must equal the sum, over every observed day, of that day's positive deviation part plus its negative deviation part.

query: We're testing a concentration guardrail on the two largest holdings together — ALU and AMA combined held to no more than 30% of the book. Where does the minimum tracking deviation land with that in place?

query: Suppose we mandate a broadly diversified book — no single stock allowed above 10% of the portfolio. What's the optimizer's best tracking deviation under that blanket position limit?
expected_pyomo: model.position_limit = Constraint(model.s, rule=lambda m, s: m.x[s] <= 0.10)

query: The top two names, ALU and AMA, together eat up nearly half the portfolio. Shouldn't their combined weight stay under 25% so we're not so lopsided? What's the tradeoff the model is making there?

query: This book leans on a handful of large positions. Couldn't we just cap every holding at 8% and let it spread out properly? What's the model gaining by concentrating instead?
expected_pyomo: model.diversify = Constraint(model.s, rule=lambda m, s: m.x[s] <= 0.08)
